title: Creating Branches
intro: You're working on a blog app and want to add a dark mode feature. Instead of changing the main code directly, you'll create a separate branch to work on safely.

teach.title: Creating a Branch = Opening a New Tab
teach.body: Think of creating a branch like opening a new browser tab. Your original tab stays exactly where it is -- nothing changes. The new tab starts from the same page, but now you can navigate somewhere completely different without losing your place. Creating a Git branch is just as instant and lightweight.

teach.title: How Branch Creation Works
teach.body: When you create a branch, Git doesn't copy your files. It just creates a tiny bookmark (called a **pointer**) that says "this branch starts here." It happens instantly and takes almost no space. That's why developers create branches constantly -- they're cheap and fast.

teach.title: Branches = Sticky Notes on a Timeline
teach.body: Imagine a long timeline of your project's history pinned to a wall. A branch is just a sticky note you place at one point on that timeline saying "start building from here." You can stick as many notes as you want -- they weigh nothing and take up no space. That's literally what Git does: a branch is a tiny pointer, not a copy of your files.

teach.title: The Commands for Branching
teach.code:
git branch                       # List all branches
git branch feature/dark-mode     # Create a new branch
git checkout feature/dark-mode   # Switch to it

# Or do both at once:
git checkout -b feature/dark-mode
teach.annotation: The shortcut `git checkout -b <name>` creates AND switches in one command. You'll use this a lot.

teach.title: Creating vs Switching
teach.body: **Important:** `git branch <name>` only creates the branch -- it does not switch you to it. You're still on your old branch until you run `git checkout <name>`. The asterisk (*) next to a branch name in `git branch` output tells you which branch you're currently on.

Creating branches is the foundation of professional Git workflow. Every open-source contribution, every team project, every AI-built app you want to ship -- it all starts with creating a branch. This one skill unlocks pull requests, code reviews, and real team collaboration.
Create a branch with `git branch <name>`, switch to it with `git checkout <name>`, or do both at once with `git checkout -b <name>`.

steps.instruction: First, check what branch you're currently on.
steps.hint: Type 'git branch' to see all branches. The asterisk (*) shows which one you're on.

steps.instruction: Create a new branch called 'feature/dark-mode' for your dark mode work.
steps.hint: Use 'git branch' followed by the name of the new branch: git branch feature/dark-mode

steps.instruction: List branches again to confirm your new branch was created.
steps.hint: Type 'git branch' to see all branches. Notice you're still on main (the asterisk).

steps.instruction: Switch to your new branch so your changes will be saved there instead of on main.
steps.hint: Use 'git checkout' or 'git switch' followed by the branch name to move to it.

steps.instruction: Verify you're now on the feature branch.
steps.hint: Type 'git branch' -- the asterisk should now be next to feature/dark-mode.

exercise.completionMessage: You've created and switched to a feature branch! Any commits you make now will only exist on 'feature/dark-mode'. The main branch stays untouched until you're ready to merge. Pro tip: 'git checkout -b feature/dark-mode' creates AND switches in one command.
